sets.title: Aspect Particles: 了 / 着 / 过
sets.description: Mandarin has no tense morphology but three aspect particles that mark the viewpoint on an event: 了 (completion or change of state), 着 (ongoing/durative state), 过 (experiential — occurred at some point in life).

pairs.label_b: I have (at some point) eaten Peking duck
pairs.question: Which sentence tells us about a life experience rather than a specific recent event?
pairs.explanation: «吃了» (completion aspect) reports a finished action, often with relevance to now — 'I've eaten, so I'm not hungry.' «吃过» (experiential aspect) reports a life experience without specifying when — 'I've had Peking duck before.' 过 is typically used when someone asks about your experiences, not when reporting what just happened.

pairs.label_b: unnatural — 了 incompatible with simultaneous state
pairs.question: Which sentence naturally describes someone who is standing and reading at the same time?
pairs.explanation: «站着» uses the durative aspect 着 to describe a simultaneous background state: he stood (and was still standing) while reading. «站了» sounds odd here because 了 marks a change of state or completion, not a sustained background posture. 着 is the particle for concurrent, persistent states.

sets.title: 把 Construction (Disposal)
sets.description: The 把 (bǎ) construction fronts the affected object before the verb to emphasize what happens to it. It requires a resultative or directional complement — the object must be 'disposed of' in some way.

pairs.label_a: I put the book on the table (把-construction: object fronted)
pairs.question: Which sentence uses the natural Mandarin word order to say 'I put the book on the table'?
pairs.explanation: «把书放在桌子上» is the natural construction when the verb has a directional/resultative complement (放在…上). Without 把, placing the location phrase after the verb requires a different structure. The 把-construction is not optional when the verb indicates a transformation or placement of the object — omitting it here sounds clumsy.

pairs.question: Which sentence places more emphasis on what happens to the door?
pairs.explanation: Both are grammatical and natural. «把门关上» foregrounds the door as the entity being disposed of; 把 focuses attention on the door's change of state (from open to shut). «关上门» follows standard SVO order. In commands, 把 often appears when the action is more deliberate or when a specific object is implied.

sets.title: 被 Passive (Adversative)
sets.description: 被 (bèi) marks passive constructions and typically carries an adversative (unfortunate or unexpected) connotation. Using 被 for positive events sounds ironic or marked.

pairs.label_b: my phone was found (positive — 被 sounds marked)
pairs.question: Which sentence uses 被 in its most natural, expected context?
pairs.explanation: «被偷了» — phone was stolen — is the classic 被 context: an undesirable action suffered by the subject. «被找到了» — phone was found — is grammatically fine but sounds mildly unusual because it uses 被 for a positive outcome. Native speakers more naturally say 手机找到了 (the phone was found) without 被 when the event is welcome.

sets.title: Topic Prominence
sets.description: Mandarin is a topic-prominent language. The sentence topic (what the sentence is about) need not be the grammatical subject; it can be an object, location, or any salient element moved to sentence-initial position.

pairs.label_a: that book — I've already finished it (topic-prominent)
pairs.question: Which sentence frames 'that book' as the topic being discussed?
pairs.explanation: «那本书我已经看完了» — 'that book' is fronted as the sentence topic, signalling 'as for that book, I've finished it.' This structure is very common in Mandarin for contrasting or introducing a topic. The SVO version is equally grammatical but doesn't signal that 'that book' is the discourse topic. Fronted topics are common in response to questions like '那本书怎么样?'

sets.title: Degree Adverbs: 很 / 太 / 挺 / 有点 / 非常
sets.description: Different degree adverbs encode both intensity and the speaker's evaluation. 挺 implies pleasant surprise or approval; 有点 adds a slightly negative or reluctant tone; 太 implies excess; 非常 is neutral high intensity.

pairs.label_a: this dish is a bit salty (negative evaluation)
pairs.label_b: this dish is quite salty (neutral-to-positive surprise)
pairs.question: Which sentence implies the speaker is mildly complaining about the saltiness?
pairs.explanation: «有点咸» implies the saltiness is slightly unwelcome — 有点 commonly introduces a mild complaint or reluctant acknowledgement. «挺咸的» (with the sentence-final 的 for emphasis) presents the saltiness as a notable characteristic, often with mild approval or neutral observation. Native speakers hear a clear evaluative difference between these two sentences.
